issues.root_cause: The issue is a problem because the function 'foo' has a mutable default argument 'x' which is a list. In Python, default argument values are evaluated only once at the point of function definition in the defining scope. This means that if you use a mutable default argument and mutate it, you will and have mutated that object for all future calls to the function as well. The runtime condition that triggers this issue is when the function 'foo' is called multiple times. On the first call, the list 'x' is initialized and the value '1' is appended to it. On subsequent calls, since the list 'x' is not reinitialized, the value '1' is appended to the existing list, resulting in unexpected behavior.
issues.fix: Replace the mutable default argument with None and initialize the list inside the function
